speaker: interviewer
text: --tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were in--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were after liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Belsen after liberation, Bergen-Belsen. And mother sent my sister out to find the chaplain, and bring a Seder, and bring candles. And she didn't come back. So we were very frightened to be separated, or someone-- just, where did they disappear to?

speaker: interviewer
text: So she sent me and made me promise to come back regardless, and try and find my sister. So I walked around, and asked whoever was walking around too whether they'd seen a girl, looking so-and-so, wearing that-and-that. And they said, oh, she went into the truck. So I said, what truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you didn't want any trucks. And pointed to those trucks. So I went up the-- these wooden steps, and banged both my fists on the door. And then, a British soldier opened the door a crack and said, be quiet. We are talking to radio London. I said, I'm looking for my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, she is in there, and, is this her? Is this her? Yeah, she was in there talking to Radio London. So I left, and I told mother what Gita was up to. And they interviewed her. They were asking around, and she must have responded in English. So they said, you're just the girl we're looking for. And there was some relatives-- distant relatives in London who heard the broadcast, but didn't know from her answers whether anyone else of the family was alive. So that day, we received a telegram, please answer immediately, who else is with you? And what we can do for you, and so on. And-- but eventually, my sister came back, carrying two candles and the Seder inscribed by the chaplain, which she still has. And my mother lit candles and invited whoever wanted to come. The girls, whoever was there-- to-- this is Shabbat. And she had encouraged them to cry. It's OK. And it was very, very meaningful, and she had the wisdom and the-- what can I call it? To-- to bring normalcy so quickly, back into-- I can only call it that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we started investigating. The Red Cross came in, and we put our names on the list. We were searching for my father. And they, in turn, published lists where you can find names, and-- and so on. It didn't bring any results for us. In the meantime, my mother came down with typhoid, a terrible case of it. And through my sister she spoke to a British doctor there, Army doctor, and who arranged a place for us where he would come every day and not to go to the hospital, because the conditions in the hospital were so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So overcrowded, they couldn't place any people. And he said, he committed himself to come in every day. And my mother was-- had a very high fever, she was hallucinating. And even when she was talking, in her fever, she said, she has to take the children and bring them somewhere where they'll be safe. And she didn't eat anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that this doctor came. He had a bit of wine or something he brought in with a swab of cotton. He tried to put some liquid on her lips, to give her some nourishment. And the next-- I came down with it, and it's unbelievable how weak you get. You-- you-- I couldn't walk, I couldn't-- but the doctor came and examined me. And said she has a strong heart. She is young, she will recover. He was concerned about my mother, but she did come through it. And the first time you get up from a thing like it, you can't walk. And daylight, it's-- it's blinding. It's-- anyhow, the-- the International Red Cross arranged for us to go to Lubeck, away from the camp, where--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's in Germany, still in Germany. And we were all going to go to Sweden, our whole group. Another group was organized of Hungarian survivors, also to go to Sweden. We were not together with them. They arranged for my mother to go and recuperate somewhere in a convalescent home, [NON-ENGLISH] home, which wasn't very good for her. She-- they meant very well, but they stuffed them with whole milk and-- that was in Sweden, already. And we were, at first in Malmo, which-- which is the most southern city in Sweden, where they quarantined us and checked us for TB and all other things. And then to Gothenburg, and near there was where my mother come up, that was a-- a camp. It was an old summer camp right in the forest. It was beautiful, where they housed us. And we stayed there for awhile. And when my mother came back, rejoined us, we moved into the city. She said, look, we are almost a family, we should have an apartment. And we managed to get an-- a curator from there who went on sabbatical, rented a apartment to us. It was provided for by the Red Cross. We had no funds at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: They arranged a-- a work-- what shall I say? A [NON-ENGLISH], they called it. They organized a-- a place with sewing machines and other things, where all your talents could come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any, except for these artificial flowers. So there, a stipendium was offered to go to a girl's finishing school, and I took it. It was for six weeks. They taught you how to make button holes, and how to do smocking, which was very good, later on. And I also got to know Swedish people, their mentality. I'm interested in people, and I learned quite a bit of Swedish. I could make myself understood. I read the newspaper. And the Jewish community there, they were very anxious for us to stay, because they needed an influx of people. [NON-ENGLISH] was not very well organized, it was minimal. And they brought everything in from Denmark. You know the glorious story of-- of Denmark, how they rescued their Jews and brought them on little fishing boats to Sweden, where they survived. So Sweden was a-- was a new-- what shall I say-- crossroads for us, because we really wanted to go to Israel very badly. We didn't want to go anywhere else, or be anywhere else. Even if it meant taking a gun and defending yourself, it-- it was what we wanted to do. And again, my contacts-- my-- from the-- from my history of B'nai Akiva came. They right away contacted us, and told us who in Sweden was in charge, and so on. And we organized, and we were trained as madrichim to go on a-- illegally to Israel on a Aliyah Bet. Those were very treacherous journeys. They were very dangerous, in a way, and many didn't survive. They would drown. They-- they became sick, and-- but we wanted to go very badly. Then they started bombarding us from America. My father's youngest brother survived, and made it to America with his family. And he said, mother's going through such a difficult time, health-wise. You can't take the responsibility and go to-- on a boat like that, she wouldn't survive it. And urged us, and wanted to send papers. And we-- we agreed to go to America. And we arrived there, cried--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date? We--

speaker: interviewer
text: More or less.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when our papers arrived-- we were hoping the papers wouldn't come, and then we would have no choice but to go to-- to Israel, to Palestine at that time. We arrived in America on December the 2nd, 1946. And it's a tremendous, tremendous feeling to arrive in such a vibrant country. Generous people, the community-- I mean, our relatives, they fell apart. My uncle fell apart when he saw us. He said, now I know my brother isn't coming back. I should say-- go back to Sweden for a moment-- that I received a letter-- which I have, it's a long letter-- that tells me about Prague, and who came back and who didn't. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: From who was the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: The letter was from a girl by the name of Naomi. She was really not my first choice, but the person I tried to contact was no longer alive. So she received all the letters. And she wrote to me that she got the letter from such-and-such. And that-- and she went to our superintendent, because I had written to her several times, and she didn't answer-- that was a Czech woman. And that she didn't write because the-- she went to the authorities, and they didn't have any information about my father. And that's why she didn't want to write. And that-- she writes also that such-and-such and such-and-such, two people who were on the bunk next to my father have confirmed your worst fears. They would send out these people, were about 10 years younger than my father, that two or three groups left to work after Auschwitz, that he definitely was not among them. And therefore we have to assume the worst. And she knows that we want to know, and that's why she is writing this to us. And one of these two men who have said the [? Harish ?] joined a brother, he had a surviving brother in Boston. And on the way, he stopped in Sweden, especially, to tell us in person. So that was-- we knew when that was, in July, and that was near Tisha B'av, either the day before, or the day after, which is a fast day. And we decided that that was going to be the outside of my father. My mother went a step further, again, to show us the normalcy, the way Jewish life is conducted. We were only young kids. And she said, we have to tear our clothes, and we have to sit shiva for one hour. Not the full seven days, because so much time has passed. This is the [NON-ENGLISH], this is the law, and that is what we did. When we arrived in America, that was a fait accompli. And we first stayed with my uncle and aunt and his family for a few weeks. We didn't-- were anxious to get out on our own, eventually. My mother found an apartment. We had jobs. My-- my first job was a filing job, which I left because the man didn't allow me to leave early on Friday to-- for Shabbos. And then I worked in a yeshiva, [NON-ENGLISH], which is RJJ for short, which was a beautiful job for me. I enjoyed-- was so much more than a job, because I didn't really know--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in New York, right? We didn't mention that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in New York. We are now in New York, and I remember the placement agency, which was connected to Young Israel, asked me, why do you want this job? You have to work every other Sunday, and it's $5.00 less they're offering you than was minimum wage, this one. $0.75 an hour. And they're offering you less money, why? I said, you don't know, I want to be among children. I-- it suddenly dawned on me that this was so thrilling to me, to see a school with children, when we had lost a million and a half of our children under 16 years of age. So that was my job until I got married. I was very friendly with many people there, and they-- they were really wonderful. And during that time, I met my husband. I met Ben Hahn, who lived in Toronto, and came on business to New York. And a mutual acquaintance, more precisely my brother-in-law's father, introduced us. But I should go--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sister had married by this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I skipped forward too quickly. My sister was engaged to this fine young fellow, also of German origin, who had come to America in the '30s. His name is Fred Blumenthal, a very nice man. And my mother was, in retrospect, or maybe it's hindsight, she went through a terrible time when she realized that she was going to take my sister to the chuppah by herself. And there were other reasons. She had gained a lot of weight in Sweden with that convalescent home. She had put on weight. Her blood pressure was high. She was on the treatment. And on the day the invitations arrived, in print, to her house, she died suddenly. She was home alone. And my brother-in-law was speaking to her on the telephone, and the phone went dead. He thought there was something wrong with the phone, but my sister found her. She was at that time, 53 years old. Somehow, we said, she-- she achieved her mission. She brought us to New York, into a Jewish community. She joined a schule immediately, which-- where she's buried. And on her stone, my father is commemorated, too. She paid membership dues to the schule. I have a friend here whose father was president of the schule at the time. He said, we have people hanging on here who-- who-- for years, and-- they-- they don't-- this lady comes in, and she wants to pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Sherman ?] Shabbas, in Laurel Park. And another-- there's so many things-- that when we were sitting shiva--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, for your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: For our mother. A lady came, introduced herself, and she said, I-- and she was from the [NON-ENGLISH]. She said she has finally repaid a little bit to this family. I said, what are you talking about? She said, when-- in Poland, many years-- during the first war, her husband was a prisoner of war. Or was-- was a-- he was a soldier in Poland. And my grandmother, my father's mother, brought him food, kosher food. And said she-- she knew the name. She knew who it was, where they had been living. And there she suddenly shows up. My mother knew her. She had met her in that schule, in that [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyway, we waited till after the [NON-ENGLISH], and my sister got married. I wanted to move out, near my job, and give them the privacy that they deserved and needed. But they absolutely insisted that I stay with them. So I stayed with them for another year and a half, or two-- close to two years. I was dating, and when I met Ben, I didn't think-- I didn't dream of going to Toronto. I mean, it's an out of town, nice man. And this gentleman persuaded me to go out and-- and go on a blind date, which I never did, and I didn't like doing. And-- but he persuaded me. He says he's so lonely. He's-- he's here on business. And the rest is history. I moved to Toronto and realized--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was that? Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married on the 25th of December, 1950. It's the only time he could get off from work. So-- and we were all alone. We-- Ben's parents were in Israel. They lived there after-- they made it there after the war. Because their daughter was [NON-ENGLISH], joined the kibbutz. I really didn't dream of moving to Toronto. You know, I made such good friends. I'd gone to school and I'd learned the language. I-- I had a good job. I had such good friends. It was-- it was really wrenching to leave here. But when I arrived here, I realized that, in a way, Ben was more lonely than I was. He had nobody here, nobody at all. He had been sent to Canada-- interned in England, and sent to Canada-- into internship. And we started building our home. We were so positive, and so carefree. We were so grateful for everything. We did so well in everything we were doing. We-- we had a child. We had a son first. Crowning glory of a marriage. I named him after my father. And then we had a daughter. I named her after my mother. And we-- we joined a community with the founders of our schule. We-- a vibrant Jewish community, and our children are married, and we have eight beautiful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what more? Really no complaints, but I never thought I could talk about this so easily as I did. It's really all inside. It's-- it's amazing. Now, if there's anything you want to fill in, because we have, you know, a few minutes left, you know, if you think for a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- I'll be-- I'll be thinking of it tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you'd like to-- tomorrow will be too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come back tomorrow. So I-- I-- I would really like to introduce my husband at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll see him in a little while, in a-- in a couple of minutes, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh you will? Because he's really my best friend and partner. And we have done very well, we're very grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: --he's retired. He-- when we first met, he had a job. He was selling electrical supplies for-- various things. And he called me up one day in-- in New York, and he said, I can't afford this coming and going, we have to break our engagement and get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why we-- but later on, he built up a business. He did very well, and we sold it a few years ago. And he's retired, and we keep busy. OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm really happy to have the opportunity to bring to my husband, Ben, into this, and seeing it's a happy ending that he be part of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know some of the-- most of the matters that my wife spoke about for close to two hours, I guess. And we can only say that we have to be most grateful that things have turned out that way they have, that she survived, that I survived, in my own way, having had, of course, none of these extreme experiences. But nevertheless, the war did not pass me by without any difficulties. And I'm grateful to-- to God that we met, that we were able to shape our lives, to-- in every respect, we-- we worked-- things worked out well. We have, thank God, beautiful children, grandchildren. We were successful in what we did, to a certain extent, within the parameters that we set ourselves. And we hope that we will have some good years left to enjoy our family and be ourselves, and that's about all I can say on this matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope our grandchildren and children will be spared to any pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's what we hope and pray for. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Poland. It's of my sister, Gita, was about four years old. And I was about a year old, sitting on the table. This picture is taken in Rauschenberg, [NON-ENGLISH]. I think it was supposed to be a passport picture. It's taken with the family, My mother, my father, my sister Gita, and I in my sailor coat. I was about 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say the names of-- of your parents, their names. That is Naftali Tsvi Kartagena, my mother Berta Kartagena. And what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said 1937. This picture was taken in Gothernburg, Sweden after the liberation, in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself. This is a picture of me. It's an engagement picture taken in 1950, just a couple months before the wedding. This is a wedding picture taken on December 25, 1950. On your left is my uncle Eli and his wife, Dora, my father's brother. On the right, my sister and brother-in-law, Gita and Freddy. And we're in the middle. Here we're pictured in the park. It was a 65th birthday for me, with Ben, here in Toronto, with Ben. And on the right, is our first-born, our son Neil, and his wife, Clarissa. On the left is Shoshanna. In the middle, Schmuel, and Ezra, and baby Elan. At the time, a baby, yes. This is our backyard. You-- you see Brenda and the [? Michael ?] Frankel, our daughter and son-in-law. Here, Brenda and [? Michael's ?] children-- on the left is Sarah, then [? Ruvane, ?] Svee and Patcha.
